title: Dog Pacing Continuously at Night: Causes and Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog pacing at night? Learn about causes like anxiety or pain, urgency levels, and why video helps your vet provide an accurate diagnosis.
content_html:
TL;DR: Nighttime pacing is typically a sign of underlying pain, anxiety, or cognitive decline and warrants a vet visit within 24-48 hours. Seek emergency care immediately if pacing is accompanied by a bloated abdomen or labored breathing.

What causes my dog to pace restlessly at night?

Continuous pacing at night, often referred to as nocturnal restlessness, occurs when a dog is unable to settle into a comfortable sleep and instead walks aimlessly around the home. This behavior is rarely a choice and is typically a symptom of underlying physical discomfort, cognitive changes, or heightened anxiety. In older dogs, this is frequently linked to Canine Cognitive Dysfunction (CCD), while in younger dogs, it may stem from environmental stressors or hidden pain.

Is my dog pacing at night a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Medium. While nighttime pacing is usually not an immediate life-threatening emergency, it is a significant welfare concern indicating your dog is in distress.
  • Schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours for a full evaluation.
  • Seek immediate ER care if the pacing is accompanied by a distended (bloated) abdomen, unproductive retching, or labored breathing.

Why should I record a video of my dog pacing for the veterinarian?

  • A short video provides visual evidence of your dog’s body language, gait, and level of awareness, which is essential for an accurate diagnosis.
  • The video helps the vet distinguish between neurological issues (like getting "stuck" in corners) and orthopedic pain (such as stiff circling).
  • It captures behavior that might not be visible at the clinic because the "adrenaline" of the visit can mask symptoms.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Night pacing in dogs can stem from a variety of underlying causes requiring veterinary attention. Behavioral etiologies include anxiety, fear, and cognitive dysfunction syndrome, manifested by restlessness, disorientation, altered sleep cycles, interrupted sleep, housesoiling, and changes in temperament such as aggression or irritability. Medical differentials contributing to night pacing are metabolic or endocrine disorders. Diagnostic evaluation should include a thorough history and physical examination, potentially followed by neurologic tests (e.g., MRI) or therapeutic trials, and assessment for underlying aggression or anxiety.

Chapter: Behavioral Medicine

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1538)

faqs.question: Could my older dog have dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, Canine Cognitive Dysfunction (CCD) is a very common cause for nighttime pacing in senior dogs, as their internal clocks can become disrupted.

faqs.question: Is my dog pacing because they are in pain?
faqs.answer: Pain, especially from arthritis or internal discomfort, often worsens at night when there are fewer distractions, leading to restlessness and pacing.

faqs.question: Should I try to stop my dog from pacing?
faqs.answer: It is best to remain calm and avoid scolding your dog. Instead, ensure they are safe from stairs or obstacles and contact your vet to address the root cause.
